3: Energy has become a prerequisite of economic development for people of any nation and an enhanced quality of life. There is enormous pressure on depletable crude oil, coal and other fossil fuels due to fast industrialization and urbanization in the last few decades [1]. In recent years, interest has grown regarding the switch from fossil fuel to clean green energy, patterns of enhanced sustainability and responsible production and consumption [2,3]. The generation and usage of renewable and sustainable energy can minimize the associated environmental and socio-economic im- pacts. In particular, the usage of waste feedstock for producing sustainable bioenergy has remained a valuable perspective that has
attracted the governments and growing populations towards waste-based bio-refinery processes [4].

The processing of bio-waste via anaerobic digestion (AD) to generate biogas energy provides certain environmental benefits like treatment of waste, generation of clean renewable energy and mitigation of environmental pollution caused by fossilised energy. AD technologies to produce biogas via various biodegradable wastes are already matured globally and, now there is a greater emphasis on its conversion to biomethane and storage technologies [5,6].

Biomethane can be a viable alternative to conventional fuels to fulfil the world's insatiable energy demand. As such, it can effec- tively replace the conventional petroleum in the transportation and agricultural sectors at first instance, which will lead towards a cleaner atmosphere and a better climate due to reduction of greenhouse gas emissions. Now globally, new policies and legisla- tions have been emerging to promote and encourage the genera- tion and use of biomethane with the establishment of sophisticated

upgraded biogas (bioenergy) plants for purified CH4 production, intending to decrease the pressure on fossil fuels like natural gas [7,8]. Several technologies are emerging nowadays for the up- gradation of biogas to biomethane, which mainly involves the scrubbing and removal of CO2 fraction and other impurities of the biogas, hence increasing the concentration of CH4 up to 98%. Some available technologies include membrane separation, pressure swing adsorption, high-pressure water scrubbing and chemical scrubbing with amine [9].

The utilization of biogas as an effective source of electricity generation also relies on its methane value (%). The purification of biogas is vital for raising the power per unit volume of compressed biogas and eliminating the H2S, which poses corrosive impacts on mechanical parts of electricity generation equipment. While, the compression and storage of the gas into the cylinders are not feasible until the carbon dioxide (CO2), sulphide gases (H2S) and water vapor are eliminated [10]. The purification of biogas im- proves the methane concentration of the biogas, i.e., a fuel with a greater calorific value. Such up-gradation process also produces a significant amount of CO2 as a by-product, that can be recovered, sorted and stored for effective deployment into various industrial/ commercial processes [11]. Whereas, to improve the operational sustainability of a biogas upgrading process, it is necessary to analyze the associated environmental impacts and design an effective mitigation plan accordingly [12,13]. A study conducted by Bhattacharya et al. [14] on the water scrubbing system of biogas depicts that various determinants like the size of scrubber, gas pressure, water streaming speed, etc., when designed and managed properly, can produce 100% pure methane. In another study, Meena et al. [15] employed locally available packing materials and designed a packed bed scrubbing system that had removed 30-35% CO2. The system was compared to the one without the packed bed and it was concluded that the packed bed system is more sus- tainable for removing CO2. Similarly, Kapdi et al. [16] depicted that 30% of CO2 could be effectively removed from the biogas when bar pressure, gas flow and water flow rates are maintained at 0.47, 1.764 and 0.670 m³ respectively within a scrubbing system. The elimination of toxic impurities such as H2S, which is highly corro- sive and toxic and CO2,which reduces the calorific value, is crucial for the compliance with quality standards of eco-safe fuels [17,18]. There are many studies available on the economic and envi- ronmental performances of biogas production, its up-gradation to biomethane and storage by employing life cycle assessment. The techno-scientific literature shows that the environmental sustain- ability of biogas to biomethane systems is better than common methods of electricity and heat combustion engines [19]. The life cycle assessment (LCA) approach has been proven worthy in identifying hotspots, evaluating and quantification of environ- mental impacts and drawing up solutions [20]. Considering sus- tainability of energy systems; various studies involving LCA have been conducted using different feedstocks, site locations and different characteristics for the evaluation of biogas plants [19,21-24]. Adelt et al. [19] studied the quantification of global- warming potential (GWP) and cumulative energy of biogas upgrading system by employing the amine absorption method. Similarly, a study conducted by Patterson et al. [25] analysed the usage of biomethane in the transport and domestic sector by employing data from the Eco-invent database. Star et al. [26] compared the two carbon mineralization technologies (alkaline with regeneration and bottom ash) for biogas upgrading and identified improvements for the market utilization. In another study, Xu et al. [27] assessed the biomethane plants equipped with biogas upgrading technologies, i.e., pressurized water scrubbing, monoethanolamide aqueous scrubbing and ionic liquid scrubbing and compared the environmental impacts respectively. Di Maria

et al. [28] compared three different scenarios concerning the treatment of an organic fraction of MSW (municipal solid waste); composting, AD with electricity generation and AD with biogas upgrading by utilizing pressure swing adsorption. The description of the studies suggests that they mostly acquire Eco-invent data- bases for LCA of biogas up-gradation (scrubbing). To the best of our knowledge, the scientific literature does not report any detailed LCA study of a modern full-scale biogas upgradation system of an anaerobic digestion plant. A further peculiarity of the study is that the data is collected from an existing and operational site in a developing country [29,30]. Therefore, in such context, the present LCA study will be a valuable addition to the pool of relevant liter- ature. As such, this holistic analysis is based on the objectives underpinned as:

<list>Life cycle assessment of biogas scrubbing system equipped with novel water scrubbing technology; to analyze variant substrate- scenarios (cattle manure and potato waste in different ratios) at an operational bioenergy facility. 
Investigation of operational life cycle sustainability of this scrubbing system in terms of energy yield and biomethane upgrading for six impact categories; the relative evaluation of impact scores with corresponding literature and determining the production of onsite energy generation.</list>

<list>Life cycle assessment of biogas scrubbing system equipped with novel water scrubbing technology; to analyze variant substrate- scenarios (cattle manure and potato waste in different ratios) at an operational bioenergy facility. 
Investigation of operational life cycle sustainability of this scrubbing system in terms of energy yield and biomethane upgrading for six impact categories; the relative evaluation of impact scores with corresponding literature and determining the production of onsite energy generation.</list>
7: <header>2.1. Overview of the biogas system design </header>

The investigation of this LCA study is focused on the scrubbing phase of a modern biogas plant; that has been installed at Lahore, Pakistan, by a local industry [29]. This modern plant design consists of three domes shaped anaerobic digesters inter-connected via a fourth underground digester, i.e., up-flow activated sludge blanket (UASB). This plant can function as a continuous flow reactor or on a semi-continuous mode due to the provision of a continuous stirring mechanism at Digesters 1 and 2 [30]. The proposed modern biogas scrubbing-filtration system is used in the plant, together with effective compression and storage systems, as shown in Fig. 1.
8: <header>2.2. Design and construction of the scrubbing system </header>

Fig. 2 has portrayed the construction of a scrubbing system, consisting of three interconnected cylindrical chambers of 2.01 m (height) X 1.21 m (diameter), made of high strength moulded fibreglass composite (FRP) and further reinforced with stainless steel ribs. These are attached to a separate gas and water collection piping system for channelizing the raw biogas and water under high pressure to the internal water-based scrubbing units. Whereas, the internal scrubbing unit is divided into chambers made up of six vertically erected FRP dish type filters having 5um perforations. The FRP materials employed for construction are from Class 1, which are resistant to water, fire, chemicals and ultraviolet light. At the external opening, i.e., at the top of each water- scrubbing chamber, two sequential filtration mediums are further employed: a) an activated carbon filter b) a silica gel filter. The service life of these filters is about 3-5 years.

the bottom and cold water is flushed (pressurized flow) from the top. The high surface media of the scrubbing tower allows the counter-flow between gas and water. The raw biogas flows against the water in the column dissolving CO2 and impurities [31]. The scrubbers are supplied with water at 10 °C to remove CO2; through an incoming biogas pressure at 2.24MPa. The water passes through the adsorption media in a general water scrubbing mechanism; but in this modern design, there is an innovative addition of the desorption media. That is provided; by a packed-bed structure of ceramic rings to increase contact between the air and water. The pressure in the flash column is decreased to 0.25-0.35 MPa, as water is fed into it. Some of the gases (CO2 and methane) and other impurities are released from the water. The water containing these gases enters the desorption media, while air is injected from the bottom. The cycle continues from adsorption to desorption media, takes 1-5 min for one to complete. The decreased pressure results in zero partial pressure for CO2. The biogas is passed from the sequentially activated carbon and silica gel filters to remove sul- phides, siloxanes and water vapours. Later, the purified biogas in the form of biomethane is compressed, collected and injected into the gas cylinders, free from water vapours and impurities.

27: The four feedstocks selected for this study depicted variant re- sults; in terms of energy productivity and environmental perfor- mance of the scrubbing system (Table 1). The comparative environmental impacts are analysed under six selected impact categories described in the following sections.
28: Under the midpoint category, the climate change potential for four variant feedstocks has been depicted, which are in the range of 1-4 kg CO2 per 100 m³ of biogas. The climate change potential represents the concentration of CO2 associated with exhaust gases, with the highest percentage of CO2 leaving the desorption column. It also relates to the indirect emissions generated during the energy consumption phase. The positive values for this indicator mean that CO2 has been emitted, which leads to a negative impact on the environment, whereas negative values mean that CO2 has been saved, leading to a positive impact on the environment after the CO2 removal. Simply the CO2 scrubbed from the biogas is consid- ered neutral when mixed in water, and later it can be separated, stored and used for commercial purposes.

The CO2 potential for each of the feedstock variants considered in the study is portrayed in Fig. 4. It demonstrates the role of each variant in the carbon balance. CBM75:PW25 and CBM50:PW50 contributed more to the climate change impact compared to the

other two variants. This is due to electrical energy usage and methane losses during the scrubbing process. The GHG emissions depends on the methane yield, which includes the construction materials used, type of feedstock, efficiency of biogas, electricity source, methane losses and other factors [40].

Poeschl et al. [41] investigated CO2 emissions from different feedstocks, biogas plant operation, biogas utilization, management of digestate and concluded that the biogas production from by- products (food residues, slaughter waste, cattle manure) are bet- ter and more sustainable than the energy crops. The biogas pro- cessed into biomethane via modern scrubbing technology factually reduced the overall CO2 emissions and climate change impact [42]. The variants CBM100:PW0 and CBMO:PW100 have avoided certain conventional utilities and caused corresponding savings of -1.20 and -1.16 kg CO2. Whereas in the case of variant CBM75:PW25 and CBM50:PW50, they modelled burden of 1.11 and 1.07 kg CO2 equivalent in terms of climate change potential, the reason is that biogas upgradation has caused the highest CH4 emissions due to more heat consumption and energy for mixing-pumping processes [43]. The increase in methane slip would directly affect the climate change potential, increasing it considerably. The equivalent CO2 savings increases considerably when the methane slip is approxi- mately 0.05%, but if the value reaches 4% the process becomes no longer sustainable [44]. The intentional release of biogas will affect the climate change savings if the production exceeds consumption. It would outweigh the savings made from the replacement of alternative fuels with biogas [45]. As this involved gate to gate analysis of upgrading facility, in contrast to cradle to grave analysis, the positive effect of the analysed and assessed system was CO2 removal, hence acting as a visual CO2 sink.

While analysing the climate change potential use of feedstock ratios, CBM100:PW0 and CBMO:PW100 have caused more cumu- lative CO2 savings, so they are more environmentally sustainable as compared to CBM75:PW25 and CBM50:PW50, which have more climate change potential. When considering the high-pressure water scrubbing technology, it is possible that CO2 can be iso- lated, even stored or can be employed in industrial processes if CO2 quality is high enough [46]. A study by Florio et al. [47] concluded that scrubbing causes more climate change potential if the scrub- bed CO2 is released into the environment untreated.
29: Under the midpoint category, the climate change potential for four variant feedstocks has been depicted, which are in the range of 1-4 kg CO2 per 100 m³ of biogas. The climate change potential represents the concentration of CO2 associated with exhaust gases, with the highest percentage of CO2 leaving the desorption column. It also relates to the indirect emissions generated during the energy consumption phase. The positive values for this indicator mean that CO2 has been emitted, which leads to a negative impact on the environment, whereas negative values mean that CO2 has been saved, leading to a positive impact on the environment after the CO2 removal. Simply the CO2 scrubbed from the biogas is consid- ered neutral when mixed in water, and later it can be separated, stored and used for commercial purposes.

The CO2 potential for each of the feedstock variants considered in the study is portrayed in Fig. 4. It demonstrates the role of each variant in the carbon balance. CBM75:PW25 and CBM50:PW50 contributed more to the climate change impact compared to the

other two variants. This is due to electrical energy usage and methane losses during the scrubbing process. The GHG emissions depends on the methane yield, which includes the construction materials used, type of feedstock, efficiency of biogas, electricity source, methane losses and other factors [40].

Poeschl et al. [41] investigated CO2 emissions from different feedstocks, biogas plant operation, biogas utilization, management of digestate and concluded that the biogas production from by- products (food residues, slaughter waste, cattle manure) are bet- ter and more sustainable than the energy crops. The biogas pro- cessed into biomethane via modern scrubbing technology factually reduced the overall CO2 emissions and climate change impact [42]. The variants CBM100:PW0 and CBMO:PW100 have avoided certain conventional utilities and caused corresponding savings of -1.20 and -1.16 kg CO2. Whereas in the case of variant CBM75:PW25 and CBM50:PW50, they modelled burden of 1.11 and 1.07 kg CO2 equivalent in terms of climate change potential, the reason is that biogas upgradation has caused the highest CH4 emissions due to more heat consumption and energy for mixing-pumping processes [43]. The increase in methane slip would directly affect the climate change potential, increasing it considerably. The equivalent CO2 savings increases considerably when the methane slip is approxi- mately 0.05%, but if the value reaches 4% the process becomes no longer sustainable [44]. The intentional release of biogas will affect the climate change savings if the production exceeds consumption. It would outweigh the savings made from the replacement of alternative fuels with biogas [45]. 32: Terrestrial acidification refers to the flue gas impurities (SO2, NH3 and NO2) that cause a decrease in the CO2 scrubbing capacity and result in implications for the plant operating procedures [48,49]. Similarly, acidification causes hindrance to the biogas generation during the combustion process as well as leads to a decrease in the calorific value if not treated [50,51]. The overall scores for the terrestrial acidification potential (kg of SO2
Fig. 4. Climate change potential by various biogas scrubbing phases.
equivalent) are -1.15, -1.14, -1.10 and 0.85 for CBM100:PW0, CBMO:PW100, CBM50:PW50 and CMB75:PW25 respectively, as depicted in Fig. 5.

The decreased SO2 and NOx emissions by biogas up-gradation (scrubbing) lowered the intensity of the acidification impact. The feedstock variant CBM100:PW0, CBM75:PW25 and CBM50:PW50 have performed better than CBMO:PW100. The savings from CBM100:PW0 and CBM75:PW25 have led to a positive impact due to the avoided nitrogen, ammonia and H2S emissions under the upgrading process. Similarly, variant III (CBM50:PW50) followed the similar pattern of the variant II and have caused savings of -1.10 kg of SO2 equivalent. The balance shows a predominance of emissions of acidifying gases in the case of variant CBMO:PW100. The indirect burdens as higher heat consumption, energy use and electricity consumption have triggered more nitrogenous emissions; hence, causing environmental load for CBMO:PW100. The operation and maintenance of the system have posed the maximum amount of acidification scores due to electrical energy usage. While the discharge of volatile and soluble quantities of nitrogen, i.e., NH3-N led to terrestrial acidification [52]. Therefore, as depicted, variants CBM100:PW0, CBM75:PW25 and CBM50:PW50 have avoided the NH3-N emissions, while, CBMO:PW100 has induced more relative acidification than other substrate-variants.

34: Freshwater ecotoxicity is a midpoint category measured relative to dichlorobenzene. Fig. 6 has portrayed the freshwater ecotoxicity potential (kg 1,4 DB equivalent) for the four feedstock variants used in the present biogas production and up-gradation process.

The variant IV (PW100) has depicted the least freshwater eco- toxicity potential of 0.071 kg 1,4 DB equivalent, compared to other variants. While variants; CBM75:PW25, CBM50:PW50 and CBM100 have reflected the scores of 0.087, 0.082 and 0.080 (kgs of 1,4 DB equivalents), respectively. The biogas up-gradation produces opti- mum benefits; compared to raw biogas combustion in CHP when ecotoxicity is considered [53,54]. The construction and operational phase of the system have posed a significant ecotoxicity burden; as the FRP, steel and water component were utilized in significant quantities. The use of electrical energy during the scrubbing oper- ation has also contributed to freshwater ecotoxicity.

The sequence of freshwater ecotoxicity scores is in the order of; CBM75:PW25> CBM50:PW50 > CBM100:PW0 > CBMO:PW100. The life cycle analysis can evaluate the environmental sustainability of bioenergy systems by quantifying the potential benefits and losses based on various feedstock substrates [33]. Although the variations in the functional unit, data inputs, reference flow fluc- tuations and allocation methods have made it complex to compare the results of different life cycle studies in the biogas energy field [55].

36: Fig. 7 illustrates the water consumption potential (m³ for the feedstock variants used for the current LCA of biogas up-gradation. The variants (CBM75:PW25 and CBM50:PW50) have reflected savings of -0.0029 m³ and -0.0032 m³. The variants (CBM100:PW0 and CBM0:PW100) have posed environmental loading of 0.0038 m³ and 0.0041 m³. The water scrubbing of biogas is least sensitive to impurities as no chemicals are employed, which makes it an effective and environmentally friendly technology [34]. Generally, the water consumption for biogas ranges from 0.5 to 5 Nm3 per day [56] and 1.5-4.5 kg/Nm³ per day [18]. As such, the present study represents a modern water scrubbing system where the water consumption has been computed as 0.02 m³ per functional unit (100 m³).

The variants CBM75:PW25 and CBM50:PW50 utilized lesser water, hence causing lower wastewater discharge (0.0012 m³ and 0.0010 m³). This means the water stream is recycled continuously in a closed loop and the only water losses are due to evaporation or fugitive emissions (i.e., 1-1.2% approx.) [57]. The impure biogas in the case of variants CBM100:PW0 and CBM0:PW100 has higher CO2 contents that subsequently require high water usage; there- fore, resulting in more wastewater production. The values in terms of wastewater production have been quantified as 0.0020 m³ and 0.0015 m³. The evaporation losses or fugitive emissions are accounted as 1.5-2%. These variants posed a more burden due to higher CO2 content and more energy usage. The energy demand of the water scrubbing system depends on the volume of water and the pump efficiency [31].

38: Fig. 8 depicts the fossil depletion potential (kg oil equivalent) for the selected feedstock variants, whereas the process that causes savings or burdens are similar to that of the climate change po- tential scenario. However, the variant CBM0:PW100 has reflected most of the savings as -9.91 kg oil equivalent, CBM100:PW0 has given a savings of -9.50 kg. Whereas, the CBM75:PW25 and CBM50:PW50 have posed environmental load instead of savings. CBM0:PW100 has shown better performance in terms of avoi- ded use of natural gas and its production leads to savings in larger amounts of fossil fuel. This variant also has produced more bio- methane than other variants. The contributory benefits are due to avoidance of natural gas, as more mixing and pumping has occurred within the scrubbing facility. CBM100:PW0 has caused a saving; that is lower than CBMO:PW100 as portrayed in Fig. 8. Significantly, CBMO:PW100 has produced more biomethane and has avoided the energy involved in the consumption and less methane losses. Similar facts have been deliberated by Hauser [34] that reduction of fossil fuels and abiotic resources in biogas plants helps to improve environmental sustainability.

41: Human toxicity being an 'endpoint' category, is measured rela- tive to dichlorobenzene and is the exposure of the chemical to the natural environment where they cause negative consequences and impacts to human life [45]. As represented in Fig. 9, the release of methane from the biogas or simply the methane leakage does not pose health issues, although the hydrocarbon mixtures can cause adverse effects on the health of humans. As yet, there is no evidence regarding the interaction between methane and biogenic systems [58].

The variants CBM75:PW25, CBM50:PW50 and CBM100:PW0 have posed the largest impacts of 1.99 kg, 1.95 kg and 2.33 kg equivalent due to the diverse characteristics during the up- gradation process. Specifically, the system operation involves methane leakage and heat consumption, producing undesired

emissions of methane and nitrous oxide, causing an environmental burden. As larger the impact scores are, the more damage can be inflicted on human health. The impacts generated by the feedstock variants during the up-gradation process have significantly higher values than the benefits gained. The variant CBM0:PW100 has posed less load because of the avoided energy recovery or avoided natural gas production. Considering this perspective, there is a need to emphasize that the type of feedstock and the up-gradation technology plays a critical role in determining the biogas routes. A life cycle assessment study by Morero et al. [59] focused on biomethane use in Argentina and concluded that water scrubbing technology poses lower impacts on human health and the envi- ronment and is an economically more feasible technology than conventional biogas fed systems.

44: Table 3 lists the cumulative life cycle assessment scores for the feedstock variants according to the associated functional unit (100 m³ of biogas) used in this study. The environmental signifi- cance of the four variants has been represented in terms of five midpoint categories and one endpoint impact category. The life cycle analysis results depicted that the deployment of the modern biogas scrubbing system poses a less ecological burden as compared to its non-use. The comparison of the results of this study with other studies cited in the literature is a bit challenging. The difference lies in the selection of functional units, system bound- aries, technologies and conditions and the impact assessment methods.

For instance, the system boundary in this study (i.e., biowaste to biomethane) is comparable to the one defined by Ardolino et al. [60]. Similarly, the study reported by Pertl et al. [61] employed the functional unit of 100 m³ biogas and concluded that GHG emissions from the AD of wastes are less harmful to the climate and water scrubbing technology is environmentally friendly. However, when the system boundary is narrowed from cradle to gate, more benefits could be gained in terms of global warming potential. Florio et al. [47] conducted a relative LCA study of biogas scrubbing technology based on a functional unit of 1 m³ of biogas and a similar system boundary as presented in the study. The study concluded that the membrane separation technology has better environmental sus- tainability and lesser life cycle impacts compared to the other upgrading (scrubbing) technologies employed. The contribution of fossil resource scarcity was depicted as -0.63 kg oil equivalent, while the global warming potential was accounted as -0.43kg CO2 eq. Carnevale et al. [31] compared the environmental impacts related to three biogas utilization scenarios; internal combustion engine (ICE), molten carbonate fuel cell (MCFC) and biomethane scenario concerned with biogas upgrading (scrubbing) through high pressure water scrubbing (HPWS) technology. The functional

unit employed was 1 m³ ,common to all scenarios. The authors concluded that MCFC (molten carbonate fuel cell) has the highest score due to favourable impacts in all selected impact categories. Moreover, in biomethane scenario, the avoided impacts were due to greenhouse effect, ozone layer depletion, summer smog and energy resources respectively.

47: This study has assessed the life cycle assessment of a modern biogas scrubbing system made of fibreglass composite material. The deployment of water-based scrubbing, i.e., via perforated fibreglass composite dishes and secondary filtration via activated carbon me- dium, can effectively and efficiently reduce the CO2, H2S and other impurities like siloxanes to produce purified biomethane. Life cycle assessment scores have depicted that feedstock IBM75:PW25 (cow buffalo manure 75% + potato waste 25%) has generated the highest amount of biomethane but with significant impact potentials for this up-gradation system. The climate change potential has been signif- icantly lower because of CO2 gain in biomass conversion. The order of variants against climate change potential is found to be as CBM75: PW25 > CBM50:PW50 > PW100 > CBM100, whereas in case of fossil depletion potential the order of scores is CBM7 5:PW25 > CBM50:PW50 > CBM100 > PW100. This scrubbing tech- nology has proved to be effective in terms of overall environmental sustainability. These results can develop an insight towards policy making for the application and deployment of such scientific and empirical study in achieving environmental and energy sustain- ability via biogas generation.
